coredata.dc:description: The development of accurate battery management systems (BMSs) for electric vehicles requires precise capacity fade modeling of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) across the wide-temperature range. While existing models primarily address anode aging at normal temperatures, they often neglect cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI) formation and its coupled effects. This study presents an advanced pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) model that simultaneously accounts for solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) formation, CEI development, and lithium deposition, effectively simulating the aging behavior of NCA/graphite LIBs from −10 °C to 80 °C. The analysis reveals a capacity fading mechanism driven by synergistic interactions among multiple interface phenomena. We quantitatively distinguish the temperature-dependent contributions of SEI and CEI films to total film resistance through experimental side reaction analysis, while elucidating their underlying temperature-dependent mechanisms. The model achieves exceptional accuracy, with a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of merely 1.24% for capacity fade estimation. In addition, the capacity fade curves are utilized to estimate the charge/discharge curves, and the coefficient of determination (R 2) of the estimated curves is 0.94, successfully establishing a cross-scale correlation from the microscopic interface reaction to the macroscopic performance fade. This comprehensive coupling model offers valuable insights into full-cell capacity fading mechanisms across the wide-temperature range and provides critical guidance for developing high-precision BMSs implementations.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of accurate battery management systems (BMSs) for electric vehicles requires precise capacity fade modeling of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) across the wide-temperature range. While existing models primarily address anode aging at normal temperatures, they often neglect cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI) formation and its coupled effects. This study presents an advanced pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) model that simultaneously accounts for solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) formation, CEI development, and lithium deposition, effectively simulating the aging behavior of NCA/graphite LIBs from −10 °C to 80 °C. The analysis reveals a capacity fading mechanism driven by synergistic interactions among multiple interface phenomena. We quantitatively distinguish the temperature-dependent contributions of SEI and CEI films to total film resistance through experimental side reaction analysis, while elucidating their underlying temperature-dependent mechanisms. The model achieves exceptional accuracy, with a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of merely 1.24% for capacity fade estimation. In addition, the capacity fade curves are utilized to estimate the charge/discharge curves, and the coefficient of determination (R 2) of the estimated curves is 0.94, successfully establishing a cross-scale correlation from the microscopic interface reaction to the macroscopic performance fade. This comprehensive coupling model offers valuable insights into full-cell capacity fading mechanisms across the wide-temperature range and provides critical guidance for developing high-precision BMSs implementations.

ce:section.ce:para: The global energy storage sector is experiencing substantial significant transformation in the face of challenges associated with energy structure optimization, resource restrictions, and technological constraints [1]. As the foundation of contemporary energy storage solutions [2], lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have achieved extensive utilization in electronic devices, electric vehicles (EVs), and grid-scale energy storage systems owing to their superior cycle lifespan, high energy density, remarkable energy conversion efficiency, and rapid response capabilities [3,4]. Currently occupying a dominant position in the electrochemical energy storage market, LIBs continue to increase their market share as the primary element of emerging energy storage technologies [5], thereby establishing themselves as the preferred option for both present and future energy storage applications [6].

However, LIBs suffer from progressive capacity fade influenced by multiple factors. Internally, this is manifested through structural changes in electrode materials, electrolyte decomposition, and side reactions. Externally factors include charge/discharge depth, current rates, and operating temperatures [7]. Such capacity fade occurs during both active operation and idle storage periods. It not only increases operational costs [8] but also elevates safety risks. In severe cases, it may potentially lead to thermal runaway and fire incidents [9]. Consequently, capacity fade remains a fundamental challenge limiting the long-term reliability and safety of LIB applications [10]. This necessitates accurate state estimation by battery management systems (BMSs) to implement effective real-time management strategies [11].

Research has identified several key mechanisms driving LIB capacity fade, including electrode material aging, solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film, cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI) film, lithium deposition, particle cracking, and current collector corrosion [12]. Among these, SEI film growth and lithium deposition are the predominant anode aging mechanisms. The SEI film, which forms during the initial cycling as a passivation layer from electrolyte decomposition products, initially benefits battery performance by preventing direct electrode-electrolyte contact [13]. However, its formation irreversibly consumes lithium ions, resulting in first-cycle capacity loss. Subsequent cycling leads to SEI film fracture and continuous thickening, which further depletes active lithium and increases internal resistance [14].

Lithium deposition, another critical fading mechanism, occurs when lithium ions are directly reduced to metallic lithium on the anode surface rather than being properly intercalated. This phenomenon not only causes irreversible loss of active lithium but also poses serious safety risks through dendrite formation and potential internal short circuits [15]. Typically induced by high charging rates, low temperatures, or low state of charge (SOC) conditions [16], lithium deposition is further exacerbated by SEI film rupture, which exposes the anode surface to lithium ions [17].

Current modeling approaches predominantly focus on coupling SEI film growth and lithium deposition to simulate anode aging. However, this singular focus risks overlooking the significant contribution of CEI film formation at the cathode to overall capacity fade [18]. Such oversight leads to inaccurate representation of cathode material structural evolution and neglects critical cathode-anode interface interactions, ultimately compromising the accuracy of capacity fade estimation [19].

The CEI film, analogous to its anode counterpart, forms on cathode surfaces through electrolyte decomposition [20]. Cycling-induced CEI film rupture and subsequent reformation lead to continuous thickening, consuming active materials and contributing to capacity loss. Emerging research suggests that while stable CEI films formed during initial cycling protect cathode surfaces and mitigate harmful side reactions, film rupture accelerates these detrimental processes [21]. Current CEI-focused capacity fade models primarily address film thickness/impedance or decomposition/side reaction mechanisms, yet few studies have successfully developed comprehensive models that effectively integrate the interactions of SEI film, lithium deposition, and CEI film.

While existing capacity fade models have achieved reasonable accuracy through multifactorial approaches, several limitations persist. For instance, some models incorporate anode expansion effects but lack long-term validation data [22] or fail to distinguish between different fading mechanisms [23]. Reported accuracies vary significantly, with some achieving a 11.99% mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) at the cost of excessive computational requirements [24], while others employ empirical approaches like extended Kalman filters (EKF) for CEI prediction without proper electrochemical foundations [25]. Particularly concerning are oversimplified CEI film models demonstrating a MAPE as high as 31.63% [26].

This study addresses these limitations by developing a pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) battery model using COMSOL Multiphysics software that comprehensively couples SEI film, CEI film, and lithium deposition mechanisms [27]. The results demonstrate superior accuracy across extended cycle numbers, providing valuable insights for precise LIB capacity fade estimation, remaining useful life (RUL) prediction, and BMS optimization. Most significantly, this work bridges a critical research gap by delivering a LIB capacity fade model that accurately incorporates CEI film reaction mechanisms in a wide-temperature range.

The study centers on Li x Ni0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA), a Ni-rich cathode material that is extensively employed in electric vehicles on account of its outstand specific capacity, energy density, and cycling stability [28]. For the anode, graphite remains the material of choice owing to its superior conductivity, impressive theoretical capacity, minimal volume expansion, and cost-effectiveness [29]. Graphite's dimensional stability significantly reduces SEI film rupture and reformation events, thereby simplifying the modeling of dendrite-induced capacity fade [30]. These characteristics led to the selection of NCA cathode and graphite anode as the baseline configuration for this investigation.

The research explores the capacity fade of LIBs across a wide-temperature range (−10–80 °C), comparing experimental observations with simulation results for NCA/graphite LIBs. The simulation models a commercial 18650-type NCA/graphite LIB, adopting a standardized cycling protocol: 1 C constant-current charging (2.5–4.2 V) followed by constant-voltage charging until the current decays to 0.05 C, then 1 C constant-current discharge to a 2.5 V cut-off, with 1200-s rest intervals between cycles. Six distinct experimental datasets were collected at representative temperatures to characterize the capacity fade dynamics:
Group 1: The capacity fading behavior was investigated under 1 C charging-discharging cycling conditions (2.5–4.2 V voltage window) at −10 °C. Advanced characterization techniques, including cryo-electron microscopy and synchrotron radiation X-ray computed tomography, were utilized to analyze the aging mechanisms in both anode and cathode materials [31].
Group 2: Low temperature storage and cycling at 0 °C were utilized to compare static and dynamic conditions. The interfacial side reactions at both electrodes were characterized using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy combined with relaxation time distribution analysis, facilitating a quantitative comparison between the contributions of calendar aging and cycle aging to capacity fade [32].
Group 3: Analysis based on National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) datasets under 25 °C conditions. The batteries in this dataset employ a comparable chemistry system, featuring a Ni-rich NCA cathode and a graphite anode. The cycling protocol, which utilizes a 1 C charge/discharge rate within a voltage window of 2.5–4.2 V, also aligns with the experimental setup. This guarantees that the dataset is suitable for validating capacity fade models at 25 °C. The experimental voltage, current, and temperature data were first processed using empirical noise reduction algorithms before subsequent capacity fade curves fitting and analysis [33].
Group 4: Investigating the modification effects of composite coatings at 45 °C, with a specific focus on the coating layer's capability to inhibit interfacial side reactions at both anode and cathode. The research quantitatively assessed the coating's efficacy in improving capacity retention via systematic electrochemical characterization [34].
Group 5: High-temperature testing in a constant 60 °C environment. Periodic capacity and impedance measurements were conducted throughout extended cycling tests to systematically investigate the correlation between capacity fade and cathode performance fade through comprehensive electrochemical characterization [35].
Group 6: The extreme high temperature of 80 °C was used to accelerate the aging process. The high-temperature electrolyte decomposition behavior was characterized through elemental distribution analysis and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), revealing significant CO2 and C2H4 evolution from electrolyte oxidation [36], while cathode structural fade was evaluated using thermal stability testing and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) showing a 220% increase in total film resistance after 200 cycles.

The model analysis at 60 °C and 80 °C in this study is based on experimental data from Refs. [35,36]. In these studies, tests within this temperature range were conducted in a high-precision constant temperature environment. The experimental setup employed a closed-loop temperature control system to strictly maintain batteries surface temperature fluctuations within ±1 °C, thereby fulfilling the assumptions of ideal isothermal conditions. From the characteristics of the experimental data, the capacity fade curves at 60 °C exhibits a continuous and smooth declining trend, with no sudden drops in capacity due to local overheating. The impedance growth data at 80 °C also shows gradual variation, with no anomalous impedance jumps associated with thermal runaway. This confirms that under these tested conditions, thermal effects did not introduce additional interference to the primary mechanisms of batteries aging. The core of the model lies in the interaction between interface reactions and capacity fade. The original experiments, through rigorous temperature control, excluded additional influences from thermo-electrochemical coupling. Since the existing experimental data itself is valid under constant temperature conditions, introducing an additional electrochemical-thermal coupling module is unnecessary. The model assumptions are highly consistent with the data acquisition context, ensuring accurate capture of the capacity fade patterns within this temperature range.

The P2D model adopts a multiscale modeling approach that optimally balances computational efficiency with predictive accuracy. In contrast to computationally demanding fully three-dimensional models, this approach substantially diminishes processing demands while retaining adequate fidelity for practical utilization. Rooted in porous electrode theory and concentrated solution theory, the P2D model comprehensively encompassed all crucial electrochemical processes transpiring within LIBs, rendering it adaptable to a wide range of battery chemistries [37]. Its unique combination of computational efficiency, simplicity of implementation, extensive applicability, and reliable accuracy has positioned the P2D model as a standard instrument in LIB research.

The aging mechanism is regarded as encompassing three primary fading factors: SEI film, CEI film, and lithium deposition. Initially, the anode aging process was modeled, where the side reactions associated with SEI film formation were found to progressively influence the exchange current density I 0 during cycling [39], resulting in the parasitic current I SEI as:

Subsequently, the lithium deposition behavior was modeled. In this model, the kinetic equation governing this reaction was derived according to the evolving SEI film thickness during cycling. Similar to the relationship between Q SEI and I SEI, the relationship between the capacity loss Q Li caused by the side reaction of lithium deposition and the side reaction current I Li can be calculated as:

The growing SEI film resistance hinders lithium-ion intercalation into the graphite anode, compelling a substantial proportion of lithium ions to deposit as metallic lithium on the electrode surface. This lithium plating phenomenon leads to the irreversible depletion of the available lithium inventory within the system. Therefore, the lithium deposition overpotential η Li can be defined as:

The current lithium deposition modeling framework primarily focuses on the irreversible loss of active lithium due to metallic lithium deposition, while not explicitly incorporating partial stripping, morphological evolution, or local gradient effects. In this study, lithium deposition is treated as predominantly irreversible. This approach is supported by experimental data across a wide-temperature range, which indicate that even at room temperature, the capacity recovery rate after deposition remains below 3% of the total capacity fade. The reversible contribution is thus negligible and does not compromise the prediction accuracy.

Although the model is developed based on charge/discharge cycles at a 1 C rate, it is grounded in fundamental electrochemical kinetics and can be qualitatively extended to lower rates below 0.5 C. At these reduced rates, the decreased current density diminishes the contribution of lithium deposition, leaving the model's precision largely unaffected. However, at higher rates above 1 C, accelerated deposition and more pronounced local gradients may necessitate the introduction of additional fading mechanisms, potentially reducing the quantitative accuracy of the model. Nevertheless, given the model's focus on typical EVs charging scenarios at 1 C, it remains relevant for its intended applications. Moreover, its core kinetic foundation allows for future extensions to higher-rate conditions.

Subsequently, the modeling framework is extended to investigate the cathode capacity fading mechanisms. Regarding the cathode material, the formation and evolution of the CEI film are identified as the primary fading factors. The subsequent analysis systematically explores how CEI film characteristics influence cathode performance. The side reactions of the CEI film give rise to a parasitic current I CEI, which affects I 0. I CEI is defined as:

where λ CEI represents the CEI film thickness correction coefficient. Finally, the capacity fade was analyzed. It should be noted that the model primarily focuses on interfacial aging mechanisms and their coupling effects, while the electrolyte is treated as a homogeneous phase with constant physicochemical properties throughout the aging process. This simplification is both common and necessary within the basic P2D modeling framework, aiming to capture the primary fading mechanisms while maintaining computational tractability. Although the model does not explicitly simulate the dynamic decomposition or compositional evolution of specific electrolyte additives, the overall impact of such processes is implicitly reflected in the model parameters. For instance, the gradual increase in total film resistance, the loss of active lithium inventory, and the modulation of reaction kinetics are comprehensive outcomes that incorporate the effects of electrolyte degradation. The parameters of this model, particularly the conductivity and diffusion coefficients related to the SEI and CEI films, can be calibrated using experimental data from specific electrolyte systems. Such calibration enables the model to effectively represent the average or integrated behavior of a given electrolyte formulation, including the beneficial effects of additives that enhance interfacial stability. Consequently, although the model does not resolve molecular-scale changes within the electrolyte, it remains a powerful tool for predicting macroscopic performance fade resulting from complex interactions between the electrodes and the electrolyte. Future work will be dedicated to integrating more explicit pathways of electrolyte decomposition and the role of additives, thereby further enhancing the model's fundamentality and predictive capability across a wider range of operating conditions.

This study utilizes the state of health (SOH) as the crucial metric for quantifying the capacity fade of LIBs. Defined as the ratio of the current capacity to the initial capacity, SOH offers a direct and quantifiable indicator of LIB performance fade [41]. Therefore, the SOH of the model can be expressed as:

To ensure the unique identifiability of the model's core parameters, this study conducts sensitivity and correlation analysis on κ SEI, κ CEI, M CEI, ρ CEI, and D s. The Sobol method was employed for sensitivity analysis, where S 1 denotes the first-order sensitivity indices and S tot represents the total-order sensitivity indices. The results are summarized in Table 1 .

The parameters κ SEI, κ CEI, and D s exhibit significant influences. κ CEI serves as the dominant factor influencing high-temperature impedance, κ SEI primarily governs low-temperature resistance, while D s modulates the Li + diffusion kinetics across varying temperatures. In contrast, M CEI and ρ CEI demonstrate comparatively lower sensitivity and exhibit minor effects on the thickness of the CEI film. To ensure parameter identifiability and preclude multicollinearity, we computed the Wilson correlation matrix for the core parameters. The results indicate that all parameters exhibit weak linear correlations. For instance, the r between κ SEI and κ CEI is 0.12 during the modeling process, which is attributable to their dependence on distinct conduction mechanisms. The core parameters remain relatively independent, confirming the absence of multicollinearity.

Fig. 1 depicts the temperature-dependent characteristics of overpotential fade rates. The overpotential exhibits nonlinear fade during cycling, with an accelerated fade observed at high temperatures (60 °C, 80 °C). Under such conditions, the fade is predominantly regulated by synergistic effects between SEI (graphite anode) film and CEI (NCA cathode) film. Although these films from different electrodes, they share similar interfacial reaction mechanisms. High temperatures promote rapid film growth and induce cyclic fracture-regeneration processes due to the combined reversible and irreversible particle expansion [42]. These parasitic reactions not only deplete the active lithium inventory and accelerate capacity fade, but also lead to a reduction in electrolyte concentration. Moreover, the instability of the film promotes electrolyte oxidation and decomposition, generating gaseous byproduct such as CO2, which contribute to irreversible mechanical expansion, premature battery failure, and potential thermal runaway risks [43]. At room temperatures (25 °C and 45 °C), the SEI and CEI films maintain near-equilibrium states, resulting in gradual overpotential fade without abrupt variations. This condition represents the optimal operational state for NCA/graphite LIBs, where the rates of side reactions remain moderate. Under low temperatures (−10 °C and 0 °C), the stable CEI film passivates cathodes and suppresses side reactions. Here, the overpotential fade is primarily governed by SEI film and lithium deposition. Reduced SEI thickening at low temperatures diminishes its impact on capacity fade [44]. On the one hand, the conductivity of SEI film is low at low temperatures. With the stable thickening of SEI film, the diffusion resistance of lithium ions increases, and the migration rate of lithium ions decreases [45]. On the other hand, the viscosity of the electrolyte increases at low temperatures, further reducing the diffusion rate of lithium ions. These combined effects severely restrict lithium-ion intercalation into the graphite anode, instead promoting metallic lithium deposition that preferentially initiates at SEI film defects. During continued cycling, the resulting dendrite growth can ultimately penetrate the separator membranes, potentially leading to internal short circuits and thermal runaway events [46].

The temperature-dependent overpotential fade profiles in Fig. 1 offer crucial insights into both thermal effects on capacity fade and the dominant fading mechanisms across different temperature ranges. However, the analysis cannot independently quantify the individual contributions of SEI and CEI films to parasitic reactions. To overcome this limitation, our study systematically evaluates their respective impacts on capacity fade by analyzing their proportional contributions to the total interfacial resistance at different temperatures.

In Table 2 , τ SEI, τ CEI, f SEI, f CEI, A SEI, and A CEI, represent the relaxation time, frequency, and peak area proportion of the SEI peak and the CEI peak, respectively. The combined results from distribution of relaxation time (DRT) and EIS analysis reveal profound effects of temperature on the electrode/electrolyte interfacial films. As illustrated in the Nyquist plot, from −10 °C to 80 °C, all impedance spectra exhibit a single, semicircular capacitive arc (Fig. 2 ). This is due to the highly overlapping relaxation times of the SEI and CEI processes, making their individual contributions indistinguishable in the Nyquist plot. The radius of this arc increases significantly with temperature, indicating a rise in total interfacial resistance, which suggests continuous thickening of both the SEI and CEI films under high temperatures due to intensified side reactions. However, DRT analysis offers deeper mechanistic insights by successfully deconvoluting these overlapping interfacial processes. Two dominant relaxation processes are identified across the entire temperature range: the high-frequency peak is attributed to the SEI, whose relative contribution (as a percentage of total peak area) decreases consistently from 98.2% to 57.0% with increasing temperature. In contrast, the low-frequency peak, assigned to the CEI, shows a monotonic and substantial increase in its contribution, reaching 43.0% at 80 °C. This indicates that the overall increase in film resistance at high temperatures primarily stems from the rapid growth and restructuring of the CEI film, fundamentally driven by accelerated oxidation reactions at the cathode/electrolyte interface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The contribution of SEI film and CEI film to the total film resistance
ce:section.ce:para: To more intuitively quantify the competing contributions of the SEI and CEI films to the total film resistance, Fig. 3 present a stacked area plot illustrating the evolution of their respective proportions with temperature. This visualization further confirms that the source of film resistance shifts from being predominantly SEI-driven to being co-dominated by both SEI and CEI films as temperature increases, providing direct evidence for the capacity fading mechanism under high-temperature conditions.

Fig. 3 depicts a comparative analysis of the resistances of simulated SEI and CEI films at different temperatures and the experimentally measured total interfacial resistance, clarifying their respective contribution ratios across various temperature ranges. The calculation shows that the overall coefficient of determination (R 2) reaches 0.96, indicating excellent agreement between the simulation results and the experimental data in Fig. 3, validating the reliability of the model. The overall R 2 is the average value of the R 2 in Fig. 3a–f. The temperature-dependence analysis reveals that as the temperature rises, the total film resistance gradually increases, and the contribution proportion of CEI film resistance also gradually elevates [47].

At low temperatures, the SEI film dominates the change of film resistance, and the contribution of CEI film to the film resistance is only 1%–2.5% (Fig. 3a and b). Although the CEI film formed at low temperatures is relatively stable, it is extremely thin and its coverage diminishes, resulting in a very low contribution to the film resistance [48]. Notably, electrolyte decomposition persists on the unprotected cathode surfaces, contributing to the gradual capacity fade [49]. The SEI film grows at a slow pace but forms an exceptionally dense film that resists fracture. However, its low ionic conductivity hinders Li+ diffusion, leading to irreversible capacity loss [50]. Under room temperatures, the CEI film's contribution rises to 5%–8% (Fig. 3c and d), reflecting a dynamic equilibrium between the two interphases. This regime is characterized by the periodic structural reorganization of both films. At high temperatures, the accelerated side reactions significantly augment the CEI film's contribution to 15%–25% (Fig. 3e and f), propelled by enhanced Ni3+ oxidation in NCA cathodes that promotes organic component decomposition within the CEI film [51]. This aligns with fading mechanisms reported for thick Ni-rich cathodes under thermal stress, where cationic disordering exacerbates interfacial instability [52]. The data further substantiates this through the significant CO2 evolution at 80 °C, indicating a critical link between cathode degradation and thermal runaway pathways. The SEI film is similar to the CEI film. The inorganic composition is reduced to form a porous structure, which leads to an increase in porosity, causing the lamellar structure of the electrode material to collapse and accelerating the capacity fade rate.

Fig. 4 presents a comparison of the voltage change rate characteristics of NCA/graphite LIBs during the aging process under different temperature conditions. The results demonstrate a pronounced temperature dependence, with voltage change rates exhibiting a marked decrease as temperature rises. Because the low temperature environment will aggravate the polarization effect (Fig. 4a and b): the increase of electrolyte viscosity leads to the blockage of lithium ions migration, and the decrease of electrochemical reaction rate leads to the increase of activation energy barrier [53]. Notably, distinct peaks emerge in both low voltage (2.5–3.4 V) and high voltage (4.0–4.2 V) regions. At subzero temperatures, lithium deposition reaches rapid saturation due to kinetic limitations, while the SEI film attains dynamic passivation equilibrium. Consequently, the characteristic peaks in both voltage regions appear attenuated [54]. As the temperature rises (Fig. 4c–f), the side reaction of the CEI film accelerates, and the cathode structure undergoes changes. Simultaneously, the anode SEI film experiences periodic rupture and thickening, and lithium deposition is inhibited. These factors result in the shift of the peak in the low voltage region and the appearance of the peak in the high voltage region [55]. Furter research reveals that gaseous electrolyte decomposition products become trapped in the cathode pores at high temperatures, creating localized contact impedance discontinuities that further modulate the intensity of the high-voltage peak [56]. The nonlinear voltage change rate profiles during different aging stages directly reflect the polarization states of LIBs. By leveraging the established positive correlation between polarization and irreversible capacity loss, this study quantifies capacity fade by analyzing the integrated area under the voltage change rate curves. This approach provides a reliable metric for assessing capacity fade under different temperatures conditions.

Comparative analysis indicates that the simulated capacity fade curves display distinct kinetic features in comparison with experimental data, generally showing either accelerated fading in the early stage or fading in the late stage. To enhance the accuracy of the model, this research integrates a correction factor to diminish the error. The correction factor was defined as μ 1 for cycle numbers less than n cor, and μ 2 for those greater than n cor, where n cor is the designated correction cycle number. The correction factor value was half of the maximum percentage error. The n cor corresponds to the critical point of electrode interface structure reorganization. At low temperatures, the SEI film thickens slowly, and n cor is related to the volume expansion coefficient K crd of graphite. At high temperatures, the CEI film rapidly ruptures, and n cor corresponds to the sudden increase in contact impedance caused by electrolyte oxidation and gas production. μ 1 and μ 2 are essentially dimensionless coefficients H equivalent experimental calibration factors, used to compensate for the errors not covered by the model, such as the nonlinear particle crack propagation and the correction coefficient λ CEI for CEI film thickness. To demonstrate the universality of the correction factors, this study adopted a leave-one-temperature-out approach. The dataset at 60 °C was used as the validation set, while the data from the other five temperature groups served as the training set. After training with the other five sets of data, linear functions of temperature were fitted for μ 1 and μ 2. Substituting 60 °C into these linear functions yielded the trained correction factors μ 1 and μ 2 at this temperature as 1.27% and −1.13%. These correction factors were then applied to the 60 °C dataset for validation. The resulting MAPE was 1.52%, which is highly consistent with the actual value of 1.24% reported in Table 3. This indicates low dependency on empirical adjustments of the correction factors and confirms their strong generalizability across different temperature conditions. The correction factor values and their corresponding MAPEs are detailed in Table 3 .

Following correction, the capacity fade curves that incorporate the effects of SEI film, CEI film, and lithium deposition exhibit a significantly higher degree of agreement with experimental data when compared to those that neglect the influence of CEI film. The comprehensive temperature-dependent simulations reveal an impressively low overall MAPE of 1.24%, confirming the model's exceptional predictive accuracy under complex multi-physics coupling conditions. As depicted in Fig. 5 , although both high temperature and low temperature environments expedite capacity fade, the dominant mechanisms vary substantially. Under high-temperature conditions (Fig. 5e and f), the side reactions of SEI and CEI films emerge as the primary influencing factors, while lithium deposition is inhibited to a certain extent. Conversely, low-temperature operation follows a dual fading pathway (Fig. 5a and b). The incomplete formation of the CEI film at the cathode results in reduced coverage, enabling continuous electrolyte side reactions, despite the existing CEI film still offering some protection. Simultaneously, the synergistic interactions between the evolution of the SEI film and heterogeneous lithium deposition at the anode contribute to irreversible capacity fade. The non-uniform lithium deposition pattern creates localized potential gradients during current flow, with depressed anode potentials interacting with elevated cathode potentials. This electrochemical environment promotes preferential lithium-ion nucleation at electrode weak points. Under these conditions, crystal nuclei exhibit anisotropic growth, with protrusions developing tip-enhanced advantages that ultimately evolve into hazardous needle-like dendrites [57]. Given their potential to penetrate separators and initiate thermal runaway, numerous studies now regard lithium dendrite formation as both the secondary inflection point in capacity fade curves and a crucial indicator for determining the end of LIBs life [58].

The quantified significant capacity fade at high temperatures highlights the considerable challenge in achieving long-term cycling durability for Ni-rich NCA/graphite cylindrical LIBs under thermal stress. Simulated results paint a starkly different picture of cycle life across temperature ranges (see Fig. 5). While the NCA/graphite LIBs can sustain over 2000 cycles at 25 °C in practical situations, their lifespan plummets to roughly 600–700 cycles at 60 °C before capacity fade below 80% (Fig. 5e), and falls under 500 cycles at 80 °C (Fig. 5f). The fade in longevity under high temperatures is not incidental but results directly from exponentially increasing interfacial fading kinetics. The model points to rapid growth and electrochemomechanical restructuring of the CEI film as one of the key drivers of failure under thermally accelerated conditions. The contribution of this film to total film resistance escalates dramatically from a negligible 1% at −10 °C to a commanding 25% at 80 °C. This cathode-driven capacity fade works in concert with the continual fracture and repair of the SEI film on the graphite anode, prompted by its anisotropic volume expansion. The situation is intensified further by catalytic electrolyte oxidation on the highly reactive Ni-rich cathode surface. This oxidation produces gas, leading to pore blockage, a sharp rise in charge transfer resistance, and buildup of internal pressure, which induces mechanical stress on LIBs components. Together, these interlinked fading mechanisms deplete the active lithium inventory and compromise cathode structural integrity, ultimately triggering nonlinear capacity fade. The close alignment between simulated and experimental data underscores that extending the cycle life of Ni-rich LIBs under high-temperature conditions demands coordinated strategies to simultaneously curb cathode electrolyte interphase instability, mitigate anode volume variation, and use electrolyte formulations with superior oxidation resistance.

In this research, a 1 C current is employed for charging and discharging processes. Based on the capacity fade curves, the alterations in the charge/discharge curves at various temperatures can be estimated and compared with the experimental data.

This study employed median filtering (MF) to process noise in the charge/discharge simulation data of NCA/graphite LIBs (Fig. 6 ). By replacing each data point with the median of neighboring values, this approach effectively eliminates abrupt voltage fluctuations. A 5-point sliding window median filter is applied, where each datum point is replaced by the median of the set comprising itself and two adjacent points on either side. Based on the 1-s sampling interval of the charge/discharge data, this approach avoids the issues of insufficient denoising with small windows and over-smoothing of electrochemical features with large windows, without introducing additional parameters. Before noise reduction, in low-temperature environments, significant numerical fluctuations arise from lithium deposition and SEI film diffusion resistance, resulting in substantial voltage noise and an original data R 2 of 0.86. Under room-temperature conditions, where the SEI and CEI films maintain a dynamic balance and reaction kinetics are stable, the original data exhibit weak noise and the R 2 of 0.88. At high temperatures, intensified side reactions lead to transient voltage deviations, yielding the R 2 of 0.85.

Comparative analysis of key electrochemical features on the charge/discharge curves demonstrates that the filtering process effectively preserves critical information (Fig. 6). The initiation time deviation of the 4.2 V constant-voltage charging platform does not exceed 2 s, and the variation in its duration remains within 1%. The average voltage deviation of the 3.0–3.3 V discharge platform is within 0.01 V, with its length variation also within 1%. The timing deviation of voltage inflection points during charging and discharging does not exceed 1 s. All these deviations fall within the acceptable error margin, indicating that the filter successfully removes non-physical noise without distorting the inherent electrochemical mechanisms of the batteries. The overall R 2 of the simulated charge/discharge curves subsequent to the MF treatment reached 0.94, demonstrating a high degree of consistency with the experimental results. The overall R 2 is the average value of R 2 shown in Fig. 6a–f. Moreover, the R 2 is minimally influenced by temperature (Fig. 6). These findings corroborate validate the feasibility and high precision of the method, successfully establishing a correlation between microscopic interface reactions and macroscopic performance fade.

Analysis indicates a distinct negative correlation between the charge/discharge duration of LIBs and the cycle number. Temperature exerts a substantial impact on reaction kinetics: high temperatures accelerate reaction rates (Fig. 6e and f), leading to a more pronounced contraction of the charge/discharge curves. The increasing resistance of SEI and CEI films further exacerbates the voltage variation rate, ultimately reducing the charge/discharge duration. Notably, NCA/graphite LIBs exhibit severe performance fade at low temperatures (Fig. 6a and b), including reduced discharge capacity and cycling stability [59], resulting in a significantly shorter charge/discharge duration compared to that at room temperature (Fig. 6c and d).

The charge/discharge curves prove to be particularly valuable for characterizing the open-circuit voltage (OCV) behavior of electrodes. By establishing quantitative OCV-SOC relationships, characteristic curves were constructed. These curves not only accurately capture the dynamic electrochemical responses of LIBs but also facilitate online SOH assessment, thereby optimizing the dynamic health management strategies for LIB systems [60]. This study employed the original cycling protocol for 18650-type NCA/graphite LIBs, which consisted of 1 C constant-current constant-voltage charge/discharge cycles within a voltage window of 2.5–4.2 V and a cycle interval of 1200 s. An OCV measurement scheme was specifically designed for this protocol. After each charge/discharge cycle, an initial rest period of 1200 s was applied to mitigate concentration polarization and electrochemical polarization. Subsequently, a dynamic rest phase commenced, during which the voltage was monitored in real time. Electrochemical equilibrium was achieved when the voltage variation remained within 0.001 V per hour over a continuous 30-min window; the stable voltage at this point was recorded as the OCV value corresponding to the current SOC. Owing to slowed lithium-ion diffusion kinetics at reduced temperatures, the duration of the dynamic rest phase exhibited temperature dependence. Specifically, the required stabilization time was approximately 4.8 h at −10 °C, 3.5 h at 0 °C, 2.2 h at 25 °C, 1.8 h at 45 °C, 1.6 h at 60 °C, and 1.7 h at 80 °C. The measured OCV deviation at all temperatures remained below 0.3%. To ensure the accuracy of the OCV-SOC relationship, measurements were collected at 5% SOC intervals, which maintained the curve fitting coefficient R 2 above 0.99 and prevented fitting inaccuracies associated with sparse data points [61]. Given the nonlinear dependence of OCV on temperature, direct use of uncorrected OCV values led to cross-temperature SOC estimation error of 5%–8%. To address this, a temperature compensation scheme was developed based on full-range OCV data acquired at six temperature points. After correction, the average OCV deviation decreased from 0.03-0.08 V to 0.005 V (Fig. 6), and the SOC estimation error was reduced from 5%-8% to 1.2%. Even at extreme temperatures above 80 °C, the proposed method sustained SOC estimation error below 2%. Further improvements in accuracy could be attained by incorporating impedance corrections related to the cathode electrolyte interface. To clarify the relationship between OCV and charge/discharge curves, this study analyzed the charge/discharge curves at different temperatures in Fig. 6 and extracted the equilibrium voltage after rest as the OCV value. For instance, at 25°C (Fig. 6c), the stabilized voltage segments at the end of the charge/discharge plateaus align closely with the OCV values obtained after dynamic rest, showing a deviation of less than 0.005 V. This consistency validates the representation of OCV behavior by the equilibrium voltage segments in the charge/discharge curves, thereby reinforcing the reliability of the SOH evaluation based on the OCV-SOC relationship. Furthermore, the increased polarization observed in the charge/discharge curves under high-temperature conditions (80 °C, Fig. 6f) corresponds with the longer rest time required (1.7 h) for OCV measurement, highlighting the significant influence of temperature on electrode reaction kinetics. By correlating the voltage response in Fig. 6 with OCV data, this study establishes a cross-scale link from macroscopic charge/discharge behavior to microscopic interfacial reactions, providing experimental support for dynamic health management.

This study conducts a comprehensive exploration of the capacity fading mechanisms of LIBs (NCA/graphite), commencing with a thorough meticulous analysis of full-cell aging mechanisms. A P2D model that couples the reaction mechanisms of SEI film and lithium deposition on the anode side, as well as CEI film on the cathode side is developed for a wide-temperature range. The complete modeling methodology is presented in detail and rigorously verified against experimental data, indicating excellent consistency and broad applicability. Our research clarifies the temperature-dependent dominant fading pathways and quantitatively assesses the respective contributions of SEI and CEI films to the total side reaction film resistance. Moreover, we establish a reliable methodology for deriving charge/discharge curves from capacity fade curves, and experimental comparisons confirm the accuracy of this approach. Experimental validation at 80 °C, demonstrating substantial interfacial degradation through film resistance growth and CO2 evolution, aligns with thermal failure mechanisms inherent to thick cathodes. This correlation confirms the model's intrinsic ability to identify thermal runaway precursors without empirical adjustment. The more factors coupled capacity fade model of LIBs established in this study offers a reference for the analysis of multiscale capacity fading mechanisms of LIBs within a wide-temperature range and provides guidance for high-precision BMSs.
